generations.text:
**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time I heard your voice, and I fear that the silence may soon be eternal.

The storm is upon us, Eliza. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with fury. I have stood here on this lonely cliff for decades, watching the waves crash against the rocks, but never have I felt the weight of the world upon me as I do tonight. I do not know if I shall see dawn.

I have not written to you in five years, not because I have no words, but because I feared the words would not be enough. I have seen your letters, those thin pieces of paper that once filled my days with hope, and now they lie untouched in a drawer, as though they were never meant to be read. I have no excuse for the silence, Eliza. I have no excuse at all.

You were but a child when you left, and I was too proud to let you go. I thought I could keep you safe, that I could shelter you from the world's cruel winds. But you were never meant to be caged, and I was never meant to be the one who held you back. I have spent these years in regret, and I have come to understand that love is not in holding on, but in letting go.

I do not know if I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee at my back, the sea roiling as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The lantern burns low, its light flickering like the candle of a dying soul. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, to the past, to the silence that has grown between us.

You were but a child when you left, no older than twelve, with your mother's hand in yours and your father's shadow over your shoulder. I remember the day like it was yesterday. The sky was grey, the air heavy with the scent of rain, and you stood at the door of our cottage, your eyes wide with a mixture of fear and longing. You did not cry, but I saw the tears in your eyes. You looked to me, and I saw in your face the same look I had worn when I first took up this post—hope, and the unspoken fear that the sea might take everything from us.

You said you were going to be a doctor, that you would bring light to the world, not just the lantern that stands here. I told you that the light is not only in the lamp, but in the heart of those who tend it. But you left, Eleanor, and I have not heard from you since.

I have not spoken to you because

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee at my back, and the sea roiling with a fury that makes me fear I may not see the dawn. The storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall live to see another day. But if I do, I must tell you this—**I have never stopped thinking of you.**

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken to you, nor have you written. I have not dared to. The silence between us has grown like a wall of stone, and I have stood upon it, looking out to sea, hoping that one day, you might look back. But you never have.

I know the reason. I know the hurt you felt, the pain of a father who was too distant, too rigid, too wrapped in the duties of this lighthouse. I was a man of duty, Eleanor—of the sea, of the light, of the solemn oath I took to keep the beacon burning for all who sailed these waters. I thought that meant I was bound to nothing else. I thought that meant I could not be a father. That I could not be the man you needed me to be.

But I was wrong. And I have lived with that mistake ever since.

I have watched the moon rise over this cliff, and I have watched the stars blink out one by one as the storm gathers. I have heard the wind

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is with a heart heavy as the storm clouds that gather over the cliffs tonight that I take up this quill. Five years have passed since your last letter, and five years have felt like a lifetime. I have written to you, I have sent letters, and yet you have not replied. I do not know if you have not received them, or if you have chosen to ignore them. I do not know if you have forgotten me, or if you have simply grown tired of a father who could not protect you.

I have kept the lighthouse these many years, through every storm, every gale, every night of bitter cold and endless darkness. I have stood upon the tower, alone, and watched the sea rage and the sky weep. And yet, I have never felt so alone as I do now, with the silence of your absence pressing upon me like the weight of the waves.

The storm is coming, Eleanor. I have seen the signs in the sky, the way the wind howls like a wounded beast, the way the sea swells with a fury I have never known. I fear it may be too late to warn you, or to reach you. I have no means of sending a message, no way of telling you that I am here, that I am still here, that I am still your father.

I have thought of you often, in the quiet hours when the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a wounded beast, rattling the shutters of this old lighthouse as though it, too, knows the storm is coming. The sea roars in protest, and I find myself wondering if this night will be the end of me—or of us.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, silent years. I have not seen you, nor heard your voice, nor felt the warmth of your presence. You were a child when you left, and now you are a woman I no longer recognize. I have tried to forgive, but the hurt lingers like the salt in the air, sharp and unrelenting.

I know I have been a failure as a father. I have been too busy with the light, too consumed by duty, too afraid to reach out when the silence between us grew too wide. I thought I could wait for you to come back. I thought I could wait forever.

But the world does not wait, and neither does the sea. Tonight, the storm is coming. The winds are fierce, the waves are wild, and I fear I may not see dawn. And if I do not, I would have you know this: I have never stopped thinking of you. I have never stopped hoping for a chance to make things right.

I remember the day you left. You were so small, so full of fire

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not be the most opportune time for such a missive. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with a fury that has not been seen in many a year. I write this by the flickering light of the lighthouse, with a hand that trembles from both the cold and the weight of what I must say.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have passed since I last spoke to you. I have not written, nor have I dared to hope that you might still remember me. You were young then, and full of fire and fury, and I, I was a fool. I let pride and silence stand between us. I thought I was protecting you, but in truth, I was only keeping you at arm’s length.

I have not seen the sea in years, but tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel its presence in my bones. The waves crash against the rocks with a sound that echoes in my heart. I have always said that the sea is a fickle mistress, but tonight, I fear it may be more than that. I fear it may take me, as it has taken so many before me. I am not young, Eliza, and the lighthouse is not as sturdy as it once was. I have done my duty, as I always have, but I wonder

**April 12th, 1893**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink of regret and the quill of a man who has long since lost the courage to speak. Five years have passed since you left our home, and I have not heard a word from you since. I have tried, my dear, I have tried to reach you, to find you, to understand what it was that made you turn your back on your father, on your family, on the only home you ever knew.

I have spent these years in this lighthouse, watching the sea, listening to the wind, and trying to make sense of the silence between us. The sea is a cruel mistress, Eleanor. She does not forgive, nor does she forget. And neither do I.

Tonight, the sky is heavy with the promise of a storm unlike any I have seen in my years of service here. The wind howls like a beast, and the waves grow ever more restless. I have been through many storms in my time, but this one feels different. It is as if the sea itself is crying out, as if it knows the hour is near. I do not know if I will survive it, and if I do, I do not know what will remain of me to tell you.

I have thought of you often, my child. I have wondered if you have ever thought of me. I have heard the stories of your life from

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit here by the window, the wind howling like a wounded beast, the sea a fury of black and white, and I find myself penning these words with trembling hands. It has been five long, bitter years since we last spoke, and now, as the storm gathers strength, I am left with a heart heavy with regret, and a mind that races with the weight of what might come.

You were never the daughter I wanted to raise, nor the one I ever dreamed of. I raised you with discipline, with the belief that duty and obedience were the cornerstones of a proper life. I thought that if I was firm, if I was stern, you would grow into the woman I saw in my mind’s eye—strong, steadfast, and unshaken by the storms of life. But I was wrong. You were not the daughter I had in mind. You were not the daughter I wanted.

I have watched you from afar, as you built your own life, your own path, and I have been a shadow, a presence, not a father. I have seen you marry, and then leave, and I have watched you grow distant, as if I were a stranger to you. And in my heart, I know I am. I have been a stranger to you, and I have no one to blame but myself.

But tonight, as the storm breaks upon us, I feel something stir within me—something I have

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the storm that now churns upon the sea. Five years have passed since you left me, and I have not heard a word from you. The silence has grown colder than the nights in this lighthouse, and the wind, though fierce, cannot compare to the chill that has settled in my soul.

You were once my light, my only hope in the darkest hours. I still remember the day you left—how you stood at the door, eyes like distant stars, and said nothing. Only the sea whispered your name as the waves crashed against the rocks. I tried to call you back, but you were already gone, like the morning mist that vanishes with the first light.

I have kept the lighthouse, as I have always done, but it is not the same without you. The light still burns, though it is but a pale imitation of the warmth I once knew. The lanterns flicker, and the wind howls like a beast in pain. Tonight, a storm is coming—one that has not been seen in decades. The sky is black as ink, and the sea rages with a fury that defies the gods. I fear I may not see the dawn.

I have no regrets, Eliza. I have done what I thought was right, though I know now that I may have made a grave mistake. I did not understand the pain you carried, the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that aches with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, that old and fickle mistress, roars in fury. I fear this night may be the last I see of the dawn. Yet I must write, for I have not the strength to keep this silence any longer.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years since you last set foot in this house, and five more since you turned your back on me. I have tried, I truly have tried, to forgive you. To understand. But the sea is not so forgiving, and neither is the heart that once loved you more than life itself.

You left in a huff, as you always did. I remember the day like it was yesterday. You were so angry, so full of words and no listening. You said I was too stern, too distant, too... *old*. You called me a relic of a bygone age, a man who could not see the world as it was. And I let you go. I thought, perhaps, that if I gave you space, you would find your way back. But you did not. You vanished like a ghost, and I have been left to keep this lighthouse, this cursed beacon, and this heart that still remembers you.

I have not spoken of you to the others. Not the postman, not the

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee at my window, and the sea, that old adversary, roaring as if to mock my frailty. The lamp in the tower flickers, and I fear it may not hold out the night. But I must write—must speak, if only in ink and paper, before the storm swallows me whole.

Five years, Eliza. Five years since you left this place, and me. You called me a fool, a drunkard, a man who let the light go out in his heart long before the lantern itself. I was too proud to beg your forgiveness, and too afraid to ask for it. I thought that time would mend what words could not. But time, my dear, is not a healer—it is a thief.

I have not seen you since the day you walked out of our home with that young man, Richard, and left me with nothing but the lighthouse, the sea, and the ghosts of your laughter. I remember the night you left. The sky was black as ink, the wind like a blade at my back, and your voice—so sharp, so cold—cut deeper than any storm. “You’re not fit to raise a child, Father,” you said. And I did not answer. I did not want to. I was too ashamed.

But now, as the tempest gathers strength, I find myself wondering—

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee outside these ancient walls. The sea roars, a beast unchained, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn. Yet, I cannot let this final hour pass without speaking to you—though I know you may never read these words.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years. I have watched the sea from these cliffs, night after night, and I have wondered if you ever looked back. I have wondered if you still remember the lighthouse, the light that once guided your father’s heart, or if you have long since forgotten it, like I have forgotten how to be a father.

You were but a child when you ran away, with that fire in your eyes and that stubbornness in your soul. I tried to reason with you, to remind you of your mother, of the life we had—of the life we *could* have had. But you were not a child anymore, and I was not the man I once was. You called me cruel, and I suppose I was. But I was not cruel for cruelty's sake. I was cruel because I loved you too fiercely, and I feared that love would only bring you pain.

I have not written to you, nor have I spoken, because I could not bear the thought that my words might bring you back. You left with your own heart,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time we spoke, and yet the weight of that silence has grown heavier with each passing day. I have not spoken to you, not because I do not wish to, but because I feared my words would not be enough. I feared they would be taken as something more than they were—something I could not bear to give.

You were the light in my life, Eleanor. Even now, in the darkness of this storm that threatens to consume us all, I see your face in the flicker of the lamp, and I hear your voice in the wind. I have kept the light burning for you, as I always have, not for the sea, not for the ships, but for you. You were the reason I took up this lonely post, the reason I chose the sea over the world.

I have not seen you in years, and I do not know if I will ever again. But I write this letter with the hope that, if the storm takes me, you may one day find it among the papers in my desk, and perhaps, in reading it, you may come to understand.

I have not been a good father. I have not been a good man. I have let duty and fear take precedence over love. I have let the sea claim me more than it should have. But I do not regret the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a beast outside, rattling the shutters of this old keep as if it, too, knows the truth of what I must say. The sea roars, and I feel its fury in my bones, as though it is a mirror to the storm within me.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left this place, and I have not heard a word from you. Not a single letter, not a single message. I have kept the light burning for you, as I always have, though I do not know if it serves any purpose now. The world has changed, and I fear I have changed with it.

I have not forgotten you, my child. Not a single day has passed without my thoughts turning to you, to the way you used to run up the stairs with your books in hand, your eyes alight with dreams of the world beyond these cliffs. You were always so full of fire, Eleanor. A flame that I could not contain, and perhaps I was too proud to ask you to stay.

I know I have been a fool. I know I have been a father who failed to understand you, who let pride and silence drive a wedge between us. But I have never stopped loving you, and I have never stopped hoping that one day, you would come back.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It has been five long years since I last set pen to paper, and longer still since I last saw your face. Time, as it often does, has moved on without us, and I find myself now at the precipice of another storm—this one not of wind and rain, but of fate.

The sea has been restless these past few days, a restless that has grown into something more. The sky, that ever-watchful sentinel of the heavens, has darkened with a foreboding that speaks not of mere weather, but of something deeper. Tonight, the tempest is coming. I have seen its signs in the waves, in the howling wind, in the silence that falls upon the lighthouse as if the very world holds its breath. I have no doubt that the storm will test the strength of this old stone, and I—this wretched, stubborn man—may not emerge from it.

I have spent many years at this post, watching the sea, guiding the ships, and keeping the light burning. It is a duty, yes, but it is also a kind of solace. The lighthouse, in its way, is a lighthouse for the soul. It stands, unyielding, against the chaos of the world. And so, I have stood for many years, as I have stood for you.

You were a child when you left, and I was a man who did not know how to say

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the damned outside these ancient walls. The sea, that fickle and merciless mistress, has been restless all night, and the storm that now gathers on the horizon promises to be one of the worst this coast has seen in a generation. I pray the heavens have mercy, and that the light I've tended for thirty years may yet guide one more soul to safety.

I have not heard from you in five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years. I have written, I have sent letters, and yet you have never replied. You have turned your back on me, as though I were some shadow of a man, unworthy of your love or your time. I do not blame you, not entirely. I have been a failure, a man who could not keep the peace in our home, who could not shield you from the storms that raged within our own walls.

I remember the day you left. It was a cold spring morning, the kind that makes the heart feel as though it has been turned to stone. You stood at the door, your eyes filled with something I could not name—anger, sorrow, perhaps a little of both. You said you needed to find your own way, that I had never understood you, that I had never listened. I did not argue. I merely nodded, my hands gripping the edge of the table as though it might

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee outside these stone walls, and the sea roiling as if it would swallow the very earth. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see morning. But I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I must say what I have always been too afraid to say.

You were a child when you left. A child with fire in her eyes and a heart too quick to be bound by the quiet, the routine, the unspoken rules of this life. I tried to keep you safe, Eleanor. I tried to keep you from the world that would have taken you, and in doing so, I may have lost you.

I remember the day you left. You stood at the door of this lighthouse, the sea stretching out before you, and you looked at me not as a father, but as a stranger. You said, “You don’t understand,” and I did not. I did not understand the ache in your chest, the fire in your soul, the need to run from the shadow of this place. I did not understand that you were not content to be a daughter, but a woman, and that I had not given you the freedom to be either.

You were angry, Eleanor. I know that. You were angry at me for not letting you go, for not trusting you to find your own path

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. The sea roars outside, a beast in its fury, and I can but listen, as I have for so many nights. The storm is coming, and though I have long since learned to read the sky, I fear tonight may be the night I do not rise again.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years since you left. I have not heard from you, not a word. I have not seen your face, nor felt your touch. I have only the flickering light of the lighthouse, the wind that howls through the stones, and the silence that has grown like a second shadow over my days.

I have tried, my dear, I have tried to write to you. I have penned letters that I never sent, words that would have torn me apart if they had reached you. I have told myself that you were not ready, that you needed time, that you needed space. But time has passed, and space has not brought you back.

I have watched the sea from my tower, night after night, and I have thought of you. I have seen the stars, and I have prayed that one of them might be your eyes, watching over me. I have seen the ships that pass, and I have wondered if any of them carry your name, if any of them might be yours.

You were once my light, Eleanor. The light

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart full of regret. The wind howls like a banshee outside, and the sea roars as though it seeks to swallow the very land upon which I stand. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write, as though the very act of putting pen to paper might somehow bridge the chasm between us.

You were but a child when you left, and I was a fool to let you go. I did not understand then the depth of your sorrow, nor the weight of your silence. You were so young, so full of dreams, and I, in my arrogance, believed I could guide you, shape you, mold you into the woman I thought you should be. But I was wrong. You were not a child to be tamed, nor a daughter to be kept in the shadow of this lighthouse. You were a soul with fire in her bones, and I, a man too stubborn to let her go.

I have spent these years watching the sea, listening to its stories, and I have come to understand that some things are not meant to be held. You were never meant to be held by me, nor by any man. You were meant to fly, to find your own path, to carve your own destiny. And I, in my pride and fear, let you go.

But now

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it has been many a year since you've heard from your old father. Time, like the tides, has worn away the edges of our once-closer bond, and I fear I have been a poor steward of it. Five years, Eliza. Five years since you left the house on the cliffs, and I have not spoken a word to you. Not a single word.

I write to you now not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the weight of something far heavier: the knowledge that the storm is coming. A tempest like none I have seen in my years of keeping this lighthouse. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, it roars as though it would swallow the very earth. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, but I fear the light may not be enough to guide the ships—or to guide me through what may come.

I have not told anyone of my fears. Not the other keepers, not the villagers, not even the priest who visits once a year. I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper must be steadfast, unshaken, and above all, silent. But tonight, I find myself wondering if silence is not the only thing I have kept from you.

You were once the light in my life, Eliza. You were the child I held close,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I am. I have not written to you in five years, and I fear that by the time you receive this, I may be gone. But I must try, for the sake of my own peace, and perhaps for yours.

The wind howls like a banshee tonight, and the sea rages with a fury that makes me wonder if it is not the devil himself who is at the helm. The lighthouse has stood for over thirty years, and I have kept it lit through storms worse than this. But tonight… tonight feels different. The waves are not merely crashing—they are *roaring*. I have heard the sea speak in many voices, but this is a voice I do not recognize.

You were so young when you left. I remember the day you walked out of the house, your dress fluttering in the wind like the wings of a bird that had been cast from its nest. I tried to stop you, but you were already gone. You said you wanted to see the world, to be free. I did not understand then, and I still do not. But I have spent every day since trying to understand what it means to be *free*.

I have kept the lighthouse, not because I am stubborn, but because I have always believed that light is meant to guide. Even in the darkest of nights. Even when no one is looking. Even when no one

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the sea howling outside, the wind tearing at the shutters like a vengeful spirit, and the waves crashing against the cliffs with a fury that seems almost human. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may not end as it began. I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor, and I do not know if these words will reach you, or if they will be lost to the sea, as so many things have been between us.

You were but a child when you left, and I, a man of few words and fewer regrets. You were angry, and I was stubborn. You said I was cruel, that I did not understand you, that I had no heart. I did not deny it. I had no heart, not for you, not for the sea, not for anything. I was a lighthouse keeper, and that was all I was meant to be. I kept the light burning, and that was enough.

But now, as the tempest gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years. I have not seen the stars in weeks, not since the fog rolled in from the north and swallowed the sky. The light is dim, the lamps are failing, and the wind howls like a wolf in pain. I fear the light may go out, and with it, my soul.

You were always drawn to the sea, Eleanor.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea roars as if it would swallow the world whole, and I fear I may not see another dawn. But I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have no one else to speak to.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child with fire in her eyes and a heart too proud to ask for forgiveness. You thought I had no love for you, that I had turned my back on you when you needed me most. But I have never forgotten the sound of your laughter, nor the way you would run up the spiral stairs to see the sea at twilight, your small hand gripping mine as if it were the only thing keeping you from falling.

I have kept the light burning these many years, not just for the ships that pass, but for the hope that one day you might return. I have watched the stars for you, and the moon, and the dawn. I have prayed that the tide would carry you back, that the wind would whisper your name to me.

But you did not come. And I have not written. Not because I did not care, but because I feared what words might bring. You were not the child I raised, nor the daughter I loved. You were a storm in a teacup, and I was too proud to

**April 12th, 1897**  
**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that feels as heavy as the storm clouds gathering over the cliffs. The sea howls like a wounded beast, and the wind screams as though it knows the truth I’ve buried for five long years. I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza, and I fear this may be the last time I ever will.

You were ten when you left, and I did not stop you. I did not stop you because I believed it was for your own good. I believed that the world beyond this rock and the sea would offer you something I could not give—something you deserved. But I was wrong, as I have been so many times before.

I have spent these years watching the waves, listening to the wind, and keeping the light burning for those who would otherwise be lost in the dark. It has been my duty, my purpose, my only companion. And yet, I find myself longing for the quiet moments we once shared—your laughter, your curiosity, the way you would press your small hand against the glass of the lantern and ask me, “Why does the light shine so bright, Papa?”

I have not seen you since the day you left, but I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten the way you would sit by the fire on winter nights, your eyes wide with wonder as I told

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not from the cold, but from the weight of the years that have passed between us. The wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea roars with a fury that seems almost human. I have been here, alone, for five long years, and tonight, I fear, I may not see the dawn.

You were not here when I last saw the light on the cliffs, nor when I lit the beacon for the first time in this tower. You were not here when I buried your mother, nor when I took up this duty with a heart full of grief and a soul full of sorrow. You were not here, Eleanor. And I have not spoken to you in five years.

I know you think I have forgotten you. That I have moved on. That the sea and the wind have taken more than just my wife. But I have not forgotten. I have not moved on. I have not forgotten the sound of your voice, the way your laughter once filled this house, the way you used to run up the stairs and call me "Papa" with such joy.

I have not forgotten, Eleanor. And I have not forgiven myself.

The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than just my life. I have spent my days watching the sea, and I have come to know its moods. Tonight, it is angry. It is cruel. It

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. The lantern is lit, as it must be, and the light cuts through the darkness like a blade—both a beacon and a reminder of the darkness I have allowed to settle in my soul.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since the last time our eyes met, and I do not know if I have the strength to speak the words that have been gnawing at me since that day. I have not seen you, nor have I heard from you, save for the occasional letter that arrives like a ghost—cold, distant, and unyielding. You have chosen your path, and I have chosen mine, and though I would have given the world to walk that path with you, I have not.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

You left like the tide—sudden, unspoken, and leaving behind only the echo of your absence. I have tried to forget, but the sea never forgets, and neither do I.

The storm is coming tonight, Eliza. I can feel it in my bones, in the way the wind howls through the rocks like a wounded beast. The lighthouse stands tall, as it always has, but I fear the night may not be as forgiving. I have spent my life keeping the light burning for those who sail the dark, but tonight, I fear I may not be able to keep it for myself.
